topic: A neighbor's tree falls on their property
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound ripped through the afternoon quiet—a sickening, splintering crash that vibrated in my chest. I stumbled out onto my lawn, my breath catching in a tight, shallow gasp. Where the old oak had stood, there was now only a jagged silhouette against the bruised sky, and a sickening tangle of broken limbs lay scattered across my meticulously kept flowerbeds. My hands trembled as I approached the wreckage, my gaze fixed on the massive, ruined trunk. A low, ragged sound escaped my throat, something between a whimper and a choked sob. I sank to my knees, the damp earth seeping into my trousers, and simply stared at the devastation, unable to move, the world narrowing down to the splintered wood and the sudden, profound emptiness.

[story 2]

"Oh, no," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy, swallowed almost instantly by the wind. My shoulders slumped forward, a heavy, dead weight settling across my spine. I walked toward the boundary line, my footsteps slow and heavy on the gravel path. Every glance I cast at the fallen tree seemed to pull a fresh wave of hollowness through me. I stopped beneath the canopy of broken branches, pressing the heels of my palms against my eyes, as if trying to physically block the sight. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I clenched my jaw so hard I could feel the ache bloom behind my teeth. I wanted to scream, but no sound emerged; only a deep, internal tremor shook my frame.

[story 3]

I stood motionless at the edge of my yard, watching the debris settle. The silence that followed the impact was deafening, thick with the smell of disturbed soil and wet wood. My knuckles were white where I gripped the railing of the porch. I took a staggered step back, my eyes darting frantically from the massive, fallen timber to the distant, indifferent houses. A sudden, sharp intake of air made my chest constrict painfully. I ran a hand through my hair, pulling at the strands roughly, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. My voice, when I finally managed to speak, was a strained monotone, "It's just… gone."
